speaker: interviewer
text: The date is July 29, 1996. This is an interview with Rachel Kessler. Her maiden name is Saunders. The interviewer is Meryl Loonin. And it's in Lexington, Massachusetts, USA. The language is English. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Meryl, M-E-R-Y-L Loonin, L-O-O-N-I-N. Today is July 29, 1996. And I'm conducting an interview with Rachel Kessler in Lexington, Massachusetts, USA. And Mrs. Kessler will be wearing headphones throughout the interview to amplify my questions. Joe, I'm sorry. Can I--

speaker: interviewer
text: I just want to tell you if--

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you say your name and spell it, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rachel Kessler, R-A-C-H-E-L K-E-S-S-L-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rachel Saunders, S-A-U-N-D-E-R-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any other names that you were called, family names or nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: May the 6th, 1902.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old are you now?

speaker: interviewer
text: In which city and country were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nottingham, England.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you describe the home that you grew up in in Nottingham?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was Orthodox. We went to shul every Shabbos. The house was kosher. As soon as we became three or four years old, we were sent to Hebrew school after school. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Yontif, of course, the High Holy Days-- on Purim and Hanukkah, the children went to shul in the evening. And as they left, they were given a bag of goodies. So that was very much looked up to, and it was a great, great day for us. Otherwise, I went to public school. I started at the age of three because the school where my big brothers went was a public school. And they would take me along with them. And I was in the infant school until I was five. And then I was in first grade, and so on. When I was nine, I was intelligent enough, apparently, to be put in a otherwise-- an otherwise paying school-- private school, until 12, when I passed an examination and went to high school. High school-- I was there for four years. The last year was doubled with some college material. Then I went to college. And my hometown had a college only, not a university. So the university where I was sent to take my finals was in London. I went to the University of London for a week and took my exams and passed.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was Nottingham like at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's quite a historical city. It still is. There's a, a very old castle high up on the hill that could overlook the River Trent, which went by. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There was a theater-- there were two or three theaters. There was a central marketplace. And it no longer is the same because what was a marketplace two days a week-- and they brought stalls in and set them up and took them down again-- that was changed to have a more elegant kind of a center. And the marketplace was put somewhere else in the town. It was a nice town. It was about a-- about a, a mile's walk down to the river. My father, who was a tailor and had his own tailor shop with several men working for him, he worked very hard all the week. And on Sunday morning-- every Sunday morning, he went-- walked down to the river a mile, along the river a mile and back again home. And I was very happy-- once I remembered to go with him. And how pleased he was to have my-- this growing girl interested enough to go with him on his lonely walk. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My father didn't-- either my father nor mother had advanced education at all, though they would have benefited by them. But I remember for quite a while on Friday nights, even though it was Shabbos, there were some public lectures being given at the local college. And I took my father with me every Friday night. And it was quite a close relationship between us, I think. I've told you that when he had-- gave the five pounds for the money that Dr. Weizmann was trying to get all over England ready for trying to establish a Jewish state that he had the certificate put in my own name-- in my name. So I think that really relates that, I guess.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we had chicken every Shabbos, OK? What, what were your parents' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Emma [? Toby. ?] And his name was Alexander Levinsky. But locally, Alexander-- they couldn't understand. People began calling him Saunders. So he took that as his surname. And all eight children were registered under the name of Saunders.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where, where did your parents come from before they moved England?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was in, in England. My father came in, in his teenage-- from Lodz, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about the synagogue in Nottingham?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, to me, it was the synagogue, the shul. It was not elegant. There was a woman's gallery-- balcony, where we girls went. We could see our father from up there if we looked up-- looked down.

speaker: interviewer
text: What shall I say? There were no classrooms there. The congregation rented rooms in, in a local school where we went for our Hebrew classes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In the building-- there were no parking spaces or anything like that. People didn't have cars anyway.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was one room which would hold maybe a dozen people called the vestry room. And when there was a bar mitzvah, the bar mitzvah boy and his family went there. And they had wine and cake, or whatever, nothing elaborate in those days.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I don't see what else I could say about the shul, except it was our shul. What about the rabbi?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rabbi Kayensky, my rabbi of whom I was very fond, for some reason, he decided I was an interesting person with whom to do more advanced studies. So at the age of 13, people-- boys and girls usually left at the age of 13. After bar mitzvah age, there were no classes. I was perhaps 14 years old when he decided to teach me privately. And I would meet with him in the vestry at the shul. [LAUGHS] And eventually, when I was 15, I took a Jew's College examination, which Jews call it in London. That's where they train rabbis. And-- but I didn't go up to London. But because my rabbi had been teaching me, he couldn't supervise my doing the exam. So it was given to our hazan. Our hazan was the only hazan I ever knew. He was there years and years and years.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went to his house every day for a week and answered all the questions. One charming thing I remember about that is that one question was, when do we recite the Ten Commandments? Well, I had no idea. I mean, nobody ever told me when they recite the Ten Commandments.

speaker: interviewee
text: But 10, 10-- now, I was taking Latin at shul-- at school. Well, 10 is an important figure. So I made a guess. And I said, on Yom Kippur.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I put down on the paper. And I still remember that hazan looking at my paper and seeing what I'd put down and just smiling and smiling. He didn't say anything. That's something I remember of that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now they have a better shul. But, see, I don't whether I told you about that. When I went back visiting and I had to go to the new shul, the door was locked. Well, I was rather surprised because this was Shabbos morning. There was a kind of driveway up the side. I walked up and looked around what was going on. There was a door. So I knocked at this door. And a man came and opened the door and let me in. The door was locked except for people coming for shul-- to shul. So there was some situation there where they felt they had to keep it locked.

speaker: interviewer
text: How common was it for a, a girl to be studying under that-- under a hazan and, and prepare--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was very-- [LAUGHS] it was very unusual, to say the least. I never heard of anybody doing that. But I also taught cheder. From the age of 16, I taught the five-year-olds. And in that first one year that they were with me, they learned how to read Hebrew. And then they went to the next class next year, where they began to read siddur and learn further. But for several years, from the age of 16 until I got married at the age of 22, I, I taught the-- in Hebrew school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were your friends in Nottingham? Were they mostly Jewish children?

speaker: interviewee
text: There wasn't much time for friendship because I, I had to be at school all day, and then a lot of homework in the evening, and my Hebrew school or teaching. So that-- we had-- I had a couple of Jewish girls, some of my close friends. And then, when I got to a-- be 17 or 18 or so, there was a Jewish Girls Club formed by a lady-- a wealthy lady-- a Jewish lady of the town, who would have us meet once a week. She rented a room in a building. And one, one evening a week, the girls came. The unfortunate thing I realized later, there were no boys. But still, here with the girls, and she had programs. And she taught us to sing things. And we went in for musical competitions. They would have all the groups from the center of England meeting together and, and vying for singing things that they were assigned to. I don't see what else I can say. She had a nephew from France because the building-- the business that-- where she got money from had something in France in Calais. And a nephew of hers was spending some time in Nottingham. When I was-- wanted to go to France, when I was going to take my final exam, I had to pass the oral. And I decided I would go to Paris for a month with the Alliance Francaise that has pupils from abroad. So I asked this young man where I could eat kosher in Paris. And he told me two places. And the first was a businessman's place. And when I got to Paris, I hunted it up. And it was on the second floor, white-- white-napkin tables. At each table, two men discussing business, probably, and I-- by myself, I didn't like it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then I went to the other, which was the kosher, kosher student's restaurant. It was near the Sorbonne. And that's where I met my husband. Or he met me. I'm going to take you back to England for a moment. Can you describe your brothers and sisters and tell their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had English names. But they're also called by their Jewish names. My father would call me Rachel. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My cousins in London-- my father had a sister in London. She had girls also. They wanted me to change my name. I should be called [? Rae ?] only. And I resisted. And that was-- I guess-- I guess it was part of my makeup. When they called me [? Rae, ?] I would say, and very dignified, my name is Rachel. There were some Jewish cousins in London. we would see occasionally, too. My father would go up to London to buy what we needed for Pesach. He would take a big suitcase and buy what we needed. Some of it we could get in, in town. There was a Jewish man in town who had groceries.

speaker: interviewer
text: Does that answer your question? Your brothers and sisters.

speaker: interviewee
text: My brothers and sisters. Well, they're all dead now, except one sister I have in England. My oldest sister ran away with a non-Jew, as she was quite a young girl, maybe 18 or 19. I don't really know what date it was. And they came to this country. And it's because-- she did that because as one can think, my parents had so much grief, eventually we were saved.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Millie-- Millicent [? Malka. ?] She was [? Malka. ?] Then Wally and then Phil-- he left two sons. One of them's dead, too, now. Then came I, then Violet. And Violet, she's the one of that prize book I showed you. She-- you know, I showed you that book. And then Phoebe is still in, in England. And Ella died.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your relationship like with your brothers and sisters when you were growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: A very good family-- that was a good family, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. Who were you closest to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very close. Well, my, my mother had-- was busy cooking for so many of us. Shabbos morning, we went to shul. We, we-- four young girls, when we came-- we'd go for a walk. That was part of it. When we came home, mother would give each of us a big slice of challah dipped on the fat at the top of the chicken soup that was heating. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: When [? Malka ?] married a non-Jew, what, what was that like for the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I was young. It was just an atmosphere of-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know what. You can't even say grief. They didn't talk about it. I remember my brother Joe was very unhappy and very upset. But for myself, I can't remember very much. And my mother insisted that we-- that we write-- correspond with her. And she would write to us so that we kept in touch. I kept always in touch with my big sister so that it was easy for me when we were in danger in France to get in touch with her and ask her to send papers. Unfortunately, she had been reading in the news about how many children in Germany had been saved. And she thought that it was to save our little girl. And so we received the papers in Marseilles but only for our little girl. And I had to send a telegram and say, I need it for myself, too. And so she sent that right away. And so we came-- when we got to this country, she was waiting for us. And we went home with her until my husband got a job.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were growing up, do, do you remember any anti-Semitic incidents?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't remember anything. I must say that the high school where I went had-- the first thing in the morning after marking the register always was-- the whole school gathered in the hall. There was a very big hall. The whole school gathered there, the little ones in front, the bigger ones behind. And they had a religious service. I'm not sure if they don't do something similar today. I'm not sure. But my parents wouldn't let me be present at a religious service. So I used to have to wait in the cloakroom until I heard them sing the hymn. And when they'd finished singing the hymn, I had to come out of the cloakroom, where I stood with the hats and coats during that time, and stand at the back-- with the tall ones at the back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was the only Jewish girl in the high school. For a while, there had been a redheaded girl. Henrietta was her name. [LAUGHS] I don't know. But we were not close. And I don't think she stayed because I felt I was the only one there. But no problem. What was the name of the high school, do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was called the High Pavement Secondary School.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you decide to go to France?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was-- it was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] what's the word for it? I always had a problem with myself psychologically. I didn't think I was clever. I mean, I was getting top grades at school. But there was something wrong with me, you know. And I was afraid I wouldn't pass the, the oral. Because you have to pass the oral in a language examination. So I decided I would go. And my professor heard about this. And he kept me back after class. And he said I didn't have to go. Well, I'd made my mind up. So I guess that was the way I was, too, when I'd made my mind up. I said I was going. But, I mean, it has been shown by the ensuing history that I just had to go because that's where I had to meet that young man.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you-- what are some of your happiest memories from your childhood? Can you recall anything in particular with your family or--

speaker: interviewee
text: My little sister Violet and I were very fond of one another. We slept together because in those days, there wasn't a separate bed for each person. There would be two children to a bed. So perhaps my real childhood would be my little sister Violet.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old were you when you went to France?

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened after you met Martin Kessler?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had to go back. I hadn't passed my finals. The exam came later. But then we wrote to one another. And I still have a bag full of correspondence of those days. And so he would write. And I would write. And my mother for a while hid the letters. And then one day, she said, oh, here you are. You may as well have these. And so we just continued writing to one another. And a year or so later, I decided, well, I couldn't-- you know, I wanted to see him. Because he'd gone home to Hungary. He couldn't make it in France. He just couldn't manage it. So he went home to try and find a job there after all. And so I decided to-- I was already teaching. Because when my exam was over, I had a job teaching school. So I went and I-- so I visited him and his family. And, and that's all, I guess. And I came back. And we decided to see one another again. He went back to France. He tried in different towns of Hungary to, to get work and decided he'd better go back to France. He'd learned French well. And so we met in, in France. And then we decided we would get married. And we fixed a date in September and made all the arrangements. He got a visa for a week, I think it was, and came over to England.

speaker: interviewer
text: My father met him in London. My father took the train to London to meet him. And one humorous thing of that time, which he would have told you if he'd been telling you about this-- he finally asked my father, tell me something. The train stops at all these stations. Why are there so many towns in England called Bovril? The-- Bovril is a-- the big, big publicity. So they spoke Yiddish together. And my father had already written to his rabbi to ask who this young man was before they arranged the wedding, and received a letter back in Yiddish from Martin's rabbi saying he was a very fine young man and a very honorable family, and that Martin's father was the head of the-- president of the congregation. So that was OK. What language did you speak at home in Nottingham?

speaker: interviewee
text: English.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you also spoke Yiddish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- we understood because mother and father would speak to one another when they didn't want us to understand something, but we would understand it just the same. But Martin and I spoke French.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like meeting his family in Hungary?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very moving. They were-- they were-- they loved me, they just-- this girl coming all the way out from England. And they, they made quite a fuss of me. And I remember Shabbos afternoon, a lot of people came visiting. Martin told me afterwards people came from 50 kilometers around to see this girl who had come to see Moishe Kessler. So that was rather amusing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe your wedding in England?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, [SIGHS] I, I [LAUGHS] don't think I can. The shul was crowded because there are not many weddings going on in small congregations. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] when, when it-- when it was finished, of course, it doesn't actual-- actually last long.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Martin gets me to give me a kiss. And my, my, my father, who'd got enervated by then, he said, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. He shouldn't give me a long kiss. So Martin's never forgotten that, yeah, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. And then we came away. We had to leave that same day because the visa was up that day. And where did you live in Paris?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the first place we lived was in one of the suburbs. Then we moved into, into Paris. And we moved on the street called Impasse des Charmilles, which was not very elegant. But we couldn't afford better. And then eventually, we moved out into a house, which was nicer in the suburbs. And our baby was born-- our little girl was born. And then we moved back in-- after a while into Paris and lived again in, in Paris.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was your daughter born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: When was your daughter born?

speaker: interviewee
text: '29-- December, 1929.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Annie.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel comfortable as a Jew in Paris at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, absolutely. There was-- the-- only in 1938, I noticed newspapers being sold on the streets had some kind of a heading which was anti-Semitic. That was the first time I'd seen it. It was very impolite to talk to people about their religion. People just didn't talk about one another's religion. You didn't know what religion people were.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they had a very rude expression. If somebody asked what your religion was, they would say, [SPEAKING FRENCH], which means, and your wife, does she deceive you? That was the French. What, what was it like being married to a foreigner, given all of the feeling toward foreigners at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: It didn't seem to matter, so long as he had a job, which wasn't often. We had friends there among the French. And did we have Jewish friends? So we have some Jewish friends and some non. Nobody bothered about religion.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do for work in Paris?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did secretarial work. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Having a French degree wasn't much use to get a job in France. It was the wrong language. They had their French. But I went, and I learned shorthand and typing. And I got jobs. And the main job I got was with Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer. The-- that was their main office in France. And it was their main office in Europe because from Paris, they managed all the other countries who-- that rented Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer films. And then since I was interested in languages, I studied some languages on my own. And eventually, my job became quite a different one. I, I was given a secretary of my own. And I dictated my mail to her. And I supervised the dubbing languages. That is to say, at that time in Europe, there was a situation where the, the different governments made a law that films could not be distributed in the same way. Only, only one main one could be shown in the capital. The others all had to be dubbed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had a specialist, a very well-educated French woman, who translated the American texts into French. And I had to pass on it that it was a correct translation. We also had German. And I supervised the German. We had Spanish. I supervised Spanish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Italian, I didn't know the language, but I could judge it from my Spanish and from my Latin. Because I minored in Latin. And Hungarian-- I was doing Hungarian because, in the meantime, I had studied Hungarian. So I was really-- that was my job, supervising dubbing in different languages.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we're going to stop there for a moment to change tapes. OK.
